rules.whatItDoes: Checks for comparisons where one side of the relation is either the minimum or maximum value for its type and warns if it involves a case that is always true or always false. Only integer and boolean types are checked.
rules.whyBad: An expression like `min <= x` may misleadingly imply that it is possible for `x` to be less than the minimum. Expressions like `max < x` are probably mistakes.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for ranges which almost include the entire range of letters from 'a' to 'z' or digits from '0' to '9', but don't because they're a half open range.
rules.whyBad: This (`'a'..'z'`) is almost certainly a typo meant to include all letters.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `foo = bar; bar = foo` sequences.
rules.whyBad: This looks like a failed attempt to swap.
rules.exampleBad:
a = b;
b = a;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for floating point literals that approximate constants which are defined in [`std::f32::consts`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/f32/consts/#constants) or [`std::f64::consts`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/f64/consts/#constants), respectively, suggesting to use the predefined constant.
rules.whyBad: Usually, the definition in the standard library is more precise than what people come up with. If you find that your definition is actually more precise, please [file a Rust issue](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues).
rules.exampleBad:
let x = 3.14;
let y = 1_f64 / x;

rules.whatItDoes: This lint warns when you use `Arc` with a type that does not implement `Send` or `Sync`.
rules.whyBad: `Arc<T>` is a thread-safe `Rc<T>` and guarantees that updates to the reference counter use atomic operations. To send an `Arc<T>` across thread boundaries and share ownership between multiple threads, `T` must be [both `Send` and `Sync`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/sync/struct.Arc.html#thread-safety), so either `T` should be made `Send + Sync` or an `Rc` should be used instead of an `Arc`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for async blocks that yield values of types that can themselves be awaited.
rules.whyBad: An await is likely missing.

rules.whatItDoes: Allows users to configure types which should not be held across await suspension points.
rules.whyBad: There are some types which are perfectly safe to use concurrently from a memory access perspective, but that will cause bugs at runtime if they are held in such a way.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `await` while holding a non-async-aware `MutexGuard`.
rules.whyBad: The Mutex types found in [`std::sync`][https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/sync/] and [`parking_lot`](https://docs.rs/parking_lot/latest/parking_lot/) are not designed to operate in an async context across await points. There are two potential solutions. One is to use an async-aware `Mutex` type. Many asynchronous foundation crates provide such a `Mutex` type. The other solution is to ensure the mutex is unlocked before calling `await`, either by introducing a scope or an explicit call to [`Drop::drop`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.Drop.html).

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `await` while holding a `RefCell`, `Ref`, or `RefMut`.
rules.whyBad: `RefCell` refs only check for exclusive mutable access at runtime. Holding a `RefCell` ref across an await suspension point risks panics from a mutable ref shared while other refs are outstanding.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for incompatible bit masks in comparisons. The formula for detecting if an expression of the type `_ <bit_op> m <cmp_op> c` (where `<bit_op>` is one of {`&`, `|`} and `<cmp_op>` is one of {`!=`, `>=`, `>`, `!=`, `>=`, `>`}) can be determined from the following table: |Comparison |Bit Op|Example |is always|Formula | |------------|------|-------------|---------|----------------------| |`==` or `!=`| `&` |`x & 2 == 3` |`false` |`c & m != c` | |`<` or `>=`| `&` |`x & 2 < 3` |`true` |`m < c` | |`>` or `<=`| `&` |`x & 1 > 1` |`false` |`m <= c` | |`==` or `!=`| `\|` |`x \| 1 == 0`|`false` |`c \| m != c` | |`<` or `>=`| `\|` |`x \| 1 < 1` |`false` |`m >= c` | |`<=` or `>` | `\|` |`x \| 1 > 0` |`true` |`m > c` |
rules.whyBad: If the bits that the comparison cares about are always set to zero or one by the bit mask, the comparison is constant `true` or `false` (depending on mask, compared value, and operators). So the code is actively misleading, and the only reason someone would write this intentionally is to win an underhanded Rust contest or create a test-case for this lint.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.and_then(|x| Some(y))`, `_.and_then(|x| Ok(y))` or `_.or_else(|x| Err(y))`.
rules.whyBad: This can be written more concisely as `_.map(|x| y)` or `_.map_err(|x| y)`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `warn`/`deny`/`forbid` attributes targeting the whole clippy::restriction category.
rules.whyBad: Restriction lints sometimes are in contrast with other lints or even go against idiomatic rust. These lints should only be enabled on a lint-by-lint basis and with careful consideration.
rules.exampleBad: #![deny(clippy::restriction)]

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for expressions of the form `x == true`, `x != true` and order comparisons such as `x < true` (or vice versa) and suggest using the variable directly.
rules.whyBad: Unnecessary code.
rules.exampleBad:
if x == true {}
if y == false {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `&*(&T)`.
rules.whyBad: Dereferencing and then borrowing a reference value has no effect in most cases.
rules.exampleBad:
let s = &String::new();

let a: &String = &* s;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `&Box<T>` anywhere in the code. Check the [Box documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/boxed/index.html) for more information.
rules.whyBad: A `&Box<T>` parameter requires the function caller to box `T` first before passing it to a function. Using `&T` defines a concrete type for the parameter and generalizes the function, this would also auto-deref to `&T` at the function call site if passed a `&Box<T>`.
rules.exampleBad: fn foo(bar: &Box<T>) { ... }

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `Box<T>` where T is a collection such as Vec anywhere in the code. Check the [Box documentation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/boxed/index.html) for more information.
rules.whyBad: Collections already keeps their contents in a separate area on the heap. So if you `Box` them, you just add another level of indirection without any benefit whatsoever.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `Box<T>` where an unboxed `T` would work fine.
rules.whyBad: This is an unnecessary allocation, and bad for performance. It is only necessary to allocate if you wish to move the box into something.
rules.exampleBad: fn foo(x: Box<u32>) {}

rules.whatItDoes: It checks for `str::bytes().count()` and suggests replacing it with `str::len()`.
rules.whyBad: `str::bytes().count()` is longer and may not be as performant as using `str::len()`.
rules.exampleBad:
"hello".bytes().count();
String::from("hello").bytes().count();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of the `abs()` method that cast the result to unsigned.
rules.whyBad: The `unsigned_abs()` method avoids panic when called on the MIN value.
rules.exampleBad:
let x: i32 = -42;
let y: u32 = x.abs() as u32;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for casts from an enum tuple constructor to an integer.
rules.whyBad: The cast is easily confused with casting a c-like enum value to an integer.
rules.exampleBad:
enum E { X(i32) };
let _ = E::X as usize;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for casts from an enum type to an integral type that will definitely truncate the value.
rules.whyBad: The resulting integral value will not match the value of the variant it came from.
rules.exampleBad:
enum E { X = 256 };
let _ = E::X as u8;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for a known NaN float being cast to an integer
rules.whyBad: NaNs are cast into zero, so one could simply use this and make the code more readable. The lint could also hint at a programmer error.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `as` casts between raw pointers to slices with differently sized elements.
rules.whyBad: The produced raw pointer to a slice does not update its length metadata. The produced pointer will point to a different number of bytes than the original pointer because the length metadata of a raw slice pointer is in elements rather than bytes. Producing a slice reference from the raw pointer will either create a slice with less data (which can be surprising) or create a slice with more data and cause Undefined Behavior.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for a raw slice being cast to a slice pointer
rules.whyBad: This can result in multiple `&mut` references to the same location when only a pointer is required. `ptr::slice_from_raw_parts` is a safe alternative that doesn't require the same [safety requirements] to be upheld.
rules.exampleBad:
let _: *const [u8] = std::slice::from_raw_parts(ptr, len) as *const _;
let _: *mut [u8] = std::slice::from_raw_parts_mut(ptr, len) as *mut _;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for expressions where a character literal is cast to `u8` and suggests using a byte literal instead.
rules.whyBad: In general, casting values to smaller types is error-prone and should be avoided where possible. In the particular case of converting a character literal to `u8`, it is easy to avoid by just using a byte literal instead. As an added bonus, `b'a'` is also slightly shorter than `'a' as u8`.
rules.exampleBad: 'x' as u8

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `.clone()` on a `Copy` type.
rules.whyBad: The only reason `Copy` types implement `Clone` is for generics, not for using the `clone` method on a concrete type.
rules.exampleBad: 42u64.clone();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for conversions to owned values just for the sake of a comparison.
rules.whyBad: The comparison can operate on a reference, so creating an owned value effectively throws it away directly afterwards, which is needlessly consuming code and heap space.
rules.exampleBad: if x.to_owned() == y {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for consecutive calls to `str::replace` (2 or more) that can be collapsed into a single call.
rules.whyBad: Consecutive `str::replace` calls scan the string multiple times with repetitive code.
rules.exampleBad:
let hello = "hesuo worpd"
    .replace('s', "l")
    .replace("u", "l")
    .replace('p', "l");

rules.whatItDoes: It identifies calls to `.is_empty()` on constant values.
rules.whyBad: String literals and constant values are known at compile time. Checking if they are empty will always return the same value. This might not be the intention of the expression.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `crate` as opposed to `$crate` in a macro definition.
rules.whyBad: `crate` refers to the macro call's crate, whereas `$crate` refers to the macro definition's crate. Rarely is the former intended. See: https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/macros-by-example.html#hygiene

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes between a type `T` and `*T`.
rules.whyBad: It's easy to mistakenly transmute between a type and a pointer to that type.
rules.exampleBad:
core::intrinsics::transmute(t) // where the result type is the same as
                               // `*t` or `&t`'s

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for construction on unit struct using `default`.
rules.whyBad: This adds code complexity and an unnecessary function call.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `#[cfg_attr(rustfmt, rustfmt_skip)]` and suggests to replace it with `#[rustfmt::skip]`.
rules.whyBad: Since tool_attributes ([rust-lang/rust#44690](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/44690)) are stable now, they should be used instead of the old `cfg_attr(rustfmt)` attributes.
rules.exampleBad:
#[cfg_attr(rustfmt, rustfmt_skip)]
fn main() { }

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `#[cfg_attr(feature = "cargo-clippy", ...)]` and for `#[cfg(feature = "cargo-clippy")]` and suggests to replace it with `#[cfg_attr(clippy, ...)]` or `#[cfg(clippy)]`.
rules.whyBad: This feature has been deprecated for years and shouldn't be used anymore.
rules.exampleBad:
#[cfg(feature = "cargo-clippy")]
struct Bar;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `#[deprecated]` annotations with a `since` field that is not a valid semantic version. Also allows "TBD" to signal future deprecation.
rules.whyBad: For checking the version of the deprecation, it must be a valid semver. Failing that, the contained information is useless.
rules.exampleBad:
#[deprecated(since = "forever")]
fn something_else() { /* ... */ }

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `*&` and `*&mut` in expressions.
rules.whyBad: Immediately dereferencing a reference is no-op and makes the code less clear.
rules.exampleBad:
let a = f(*&mut b);
let c = *&d;

rules.whatItDoes: Detects manual `std::default::Default` implementations that are identical to a derived implementation.
rules.whyBad: It is less concise.

rules.whatItDoes: Lints against manual `PartialOrd` and `Ord` implementations for types with a derived `Ord` or `PartialOrd` implementation.
rules.whyBad: The implementation of these traits must agree (for example for use with `sort`) so it’s probably a bad idea to use a default-generated `Ord` implementation with an explicitly defined `PartialOrd`. In particular, the following must hold for any type implementing `Ord`: ```text k1.cmp(&k2) == k1.partial_cmp(&k2).unwrap() ```

rules.whatItDoes: Lints against manual `PartialEq` implementations for types with a derived `Hash` implementation.
rules.whyBad: The implementation of these traits must agree (for example for use with `HashMap`) so it’s probably a bad idea to use a default-generated `Hash` implementation with an explicitly defined `PartialEq`. In particular, the following must hold for any type: ```text k1 == k2 ⇒ hash(k1) == hash(k2) ```

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for diverging calls that are not match arms or statements.
rules.whyBad: It is often confusing to read. In addition, the sub-expression evaluation order for Rust is not well documented.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for double comparisons that could be simplified to a single expression.
rules.whyBad: Readability.
rules.exampleBad: if x == y || x < y {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for unnecessary double parentheses.
rules.whyBad: This makes code harder to read and might indicate a mistake.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `.drain()` that clear the collection, immediately followed by a call to `.collect()`. > "Collection" in this context refers to any type with a `drain` method: > `Vec`, `VecDeque`, `BinaryHeap`, `HashSet`,`HashMap`, `String`
rules.whyBad: Using `mem::take` is faster as it avoids the allocation. When using `mem::take`, the old collection is replaced with an empty one and ownership of the old collection is returned.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `std::mem::drop` with a value that does not implement `Drop`.
rules.whyBad: Calling `std::mem::drop` is no different than dropping such a type. A different value may have been intended.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for files that are included as modules multiple times.
rules.whyBad: Loading a file as a module more than once causes it to be compiled multiple times, taking longer and putting duplicate content into the module tree.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for attributes that appear two or more times.
rules.whyBad: Repeating an attribute on the same item (or globally on the same crate) is unnecessary and doesn't have an effect.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calculation of subsecond microseconds or milliseconds from other `Duration` methods.
rules.whyBad: It's more concise to call `Duration::subsec_micros()` or `Duration::subsec_millis()` than to calculate them.
rules.exampleBad:
let micros = duration.subsec_nanos() / 1_000;
let millis = duration.subsec_nanos() / 1_000_000;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for integer validity checks, followed by a transmute that is (incorrectly) evaluated eagerly (e.g. using `bool::then_some`).
rules.whyBad: Eager evaluation means that the `transmute` call is executed regardless of whether the condition is true or false. This can introduce unsoundness and other subtle bugs.

rules.whatItDoes: Detects documentation that is empty.
rules.whyBad: Empty docs clutter code without adding value, reducing readability and maintainability.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for empty `loop` expressions.
rules.whyBad: These busy loops burn CPU cycles without doing anything. It is _almost always_ a better idea to `panic!` than to have a busy loop. If panicking isn't possible, think of the environment and either: - block on something - sleep the thread for some microseconds - yield or pause the thread For `std` targets, this can be done with [`std::thread::sleep`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/thread/fn.sleep.html) or [`std::thread::yield_now`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/thread/fn.yield_now.html). For `no_std` targets, doing this is more complicated, especially because `#[panic_handler]`s can't panic. To stop/pause the thread, you will probably need to invoke some target-specific intrinsic. Examples include: - [`x86_64::instructions::hlt`](https://docs.rs/x86_64/0.12.2/x86_64/instructions/fn.hlt.html) - [`cortex_m::asm::wfi`](https://docs.rs/cortex-m/0.6.3/cortex_m/asm/fn.wfi.html)
rules.exampleBad: loop {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for C-like enumerations that are `repr(isize/usize)` and have values that don't fit into an `i32`.
rules.whyBad: This will truncate the variant value on 32 bit architectures, but works fine on 64 bit.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for equal operands to comparison, logical and bitwise, difference and division binary operators (`==`, `>`, etc., `&&`, `||`, `&`, `|`, `^`, `-` and `/`).
rules.whyBad: This is usually just a typo or a copy and paste error.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for erasing operations, e.g., `x * 0`.
rules.whyBad: The whole expression can be replaced by zero. This is most likely not the intended outcome and should probably be corrected

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for blocks which are nested beyond a certain threshold. Note: Even though this lint is warn-by-default, it will only trigger if a maximum nesting level is defined in the clippy.toml file.
rules.whyBad: It can severely hinder readability.
rules.exampleBad: excessive-nesting-threshold = 3

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `.expect(&format!(...))`, `.expect(foo(..))`, etc., and suggests to use `unwrap_or_else` instead
rules.whyBad: The function will always be called.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for dereferencing expressions which would be covered by auto-deref.
rules.whyBad: This unnecessarily complicates the code.
rules.exampleBad:
let x = String::new();
let y: &str = &*x;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks `for` loops over slices with an explicit counter and suggests the use of `.enumerate()`.
rules.whyBad: Using `.enumerate()` makes the intent more clear, declutters the code and may be faster in some instances.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `write!()` / `writeln()!` which can be replaced with `(e)print!()` / `(e)println!()`
rules.whyBad: Using `(e)println!` is clearer and more concise
rules.exampleBad:
writeln!(&mut std::io::stderr(), "foo: {:?}", bar).unwrap();
writeln!(&mut std::io::stdout(), "foo: {:?}", bar).unwrap();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for occurrences where one vector gets extended instead of append
rules.whyBad: Using `append` instead of `extend` is more concise and faster

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for lifetimes in generics that are never used anywhere else.
rules.whyBad: The additional lifetimes make the code look more complicated, while there is nothing out of the ordinary going on. Removing them leads to more readable code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for type parameters in generics that are never used anywhere else.
rules.whyBad: Functions cannot infer the value of unused type parameters; therefore, calling them requires using a turbofish, which serves no purpose but to satisfy the compiler.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `filter_map(|x| x)`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely by using `flatten`.
rules.exampleBad: iter.filter_map(|x| x);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.filter(_).next()`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely as `_.find(_)`.
rules.exampleBad: vec.iter().filter(|x| **x == 0).next();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `flat_map(|x| x)`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely by using `flatten`.
rules.exampleBad: iter.flat_map(|x| x);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for statements of the form `(a - b) < f32::EPSILON` or `(a - b) < f64::EPSILON`. Notes the missing `.abs()`.
rules.whyBad: The code without `.abs()` is more likely to have a bug.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for comparisons with an address of a function item.
rules.whyBad: Function item address is not guaranteed to be unique and could vary between different code generation units. Furthermore different function items could have the same address after being merged together.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `std::mem::forget` with a value that does not implement `Drop`.
rules.whyBad: Calling `std::mem::forget` is no different than dropping such a type. A different value may have been intended.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `.map(|_| format!(..)).collect::<String>()`.
rules.whyBad: This allocates a new string for every element in the iterator. This can be done more efficiently by creating the `String` once and appending to it in `Iterator::fold`, using either the `write!` macro which supports exactly the same syntax as the `format!` macro, or concatenating with `+` in case the iterator yields `&str`/`String`. Note also that `write!`-ing into a `String` can never fail, despite the return type of `write!` being `std::fmt::Result`, so it can be safely ignored or unwrapped.

rules.whatItDoes: Detects `format!` within the arguments of another macro that does formatting such as `format!` itself, `write!` or `println!`. Suggests inlining the `format!` call.
rules.whyBad: The recommended code is both shorter and avoids a temporary allocation.
rules.exampleBad: println!("error: {}", format!("something failed at {}", Location::caller()));

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for outer doc comments written with 4 forward slashes (`////`).
rules.whyBad: This is (probably) a typo, and results in it not being a doc comment; just a regular comment.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks if we're passing a `c_void` raw pointer to `{Box,Rc,Arc,Weak}::from_raw(_)`
rules.whyBad: When dealing with `c_void` raw pointers in FFI, it is easy to run into the pitfall of calling `from_raw` with the `c_void` pointer. The type signature of `Box::from_raw` is `fn from_raw(raw: *mut T) -> Box<T>`, so if you pass a `*mut c_void` you will get a `Box<c_void>` (and similarly for `Rc`, `Arc` and `Weak`). For this to be safe, `c_void` would need to have the same memory layout as the original type, which is often not the case.
rules.exampleBad:
let ptr = Box::into_raw(Box::new(42usize)) as *mut c_void;
let _ = unsafe { Box::from_raw(ptr) };

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `x.get(x.len() - 1)` instead of `x.last()`.
rules.whyBad: Using `x.last()` is easier to read and has the same result. Note that using `x[x.len() - 1]` is semantically different from `x.last()`. Indexing into the array will panic on out-of-bounds accesses, while `x.get()` and `x.last()` will return `None`. There is another lint (get_unwrap) that covers the case of using `x.get(index).unwrap()` instead of `x[index]`.
rules.exampleBad:
let x = vec![2, 3, 5];
let last_element = x.get(x.len() - 1);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for identity operations, e.g., `x + 0`.
rules.whyBad: This code can be removed without changing the meaning. So it just obscures what's going on. Delete it mercilessly.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `Mutex::lock` calls in `if let` expression with lock calls in any of the else blocks.
rules.whyBad: The Mutex lock remains held for the whole `if let ... else` block and deadlocks.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for consecutive `if`s with the same condition.
rules.whyBad: This is probably a copy & paste error.

rules.whatItDoes: This lint is concerned with the semantics of `Borrow` and `Hash` for a type that implements all three of `Hash`, `Borrow<str>` and `Borrow<[u8]>` as it is impossible to satisfy the semantics of Borrow and `Hash` for both `Borrow<str>` and `Borrow<[u8]>`.
rules.whyBad: When providing implementations for `Borrow<T>`, one should consider whether the different implementations should act as facets or representations of the underlying type. Generic code typically uses `Borrow<T>` when it relies on the identical behavior of these additional trait implementations. These traits will likely appear as additional trait bounds. In particular `Eq`, `Ord` and `Hash` must be equivalent for borrowed and owned values: `x.borrow() == y.borrow()` should give the same result as `x == y`. It follows then that the following equivalence must hold: `hash(x) == hash((x as Borrow<[u8]>).borrow()) == hash((x as Borrow<str>).borrow())` Unfortunately it doesn't hold as `hash("abc") != hash("abc".as_bytes())`. This happens because the `Hash` impl for str passes an additional `0xFF` byte to the hasher to avoid collisions. For example, given the tuples `("a", "bc")`, and `("ab", "c")`, the two tuples would have the same hash value if the `0xFF` byte was not added.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for bounds in `impl Trait` in return position that are implied by other bounds. This can happen when a trait is specified that another trait already has as a supertrait (e.g. `fn() -> impl Deref + DerefMut<Target = i32>` has an unnecessary `Deref` bound, because `Deref` is a supertrait of `DerefMut`)
rules.whyBad: Specifying more bounds than necessary adds needless complexity for the reader.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for double comparisons that can never succeed
rules.whyBad: The whole expression can be replaced by `false`, which is probably not the programmer's intention
rules.exampleBad: if status_code <= 400 && status_code > 500 {}

rules.whatItDoes: This lint checks that no function newer than the defined MSRV (minimum supported rust version) is used in the crate.
rules.whyBad: It would prevent the crate to be actually used with the specified MSRV.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for bit masks in comparisons which can be removed without changing the outcome. The basic structure can be seen in the following table: |Comparison| Bit Op |Example |equals | |----------|----------|------------|-------| |`>` / `<=`|`\|` / `^`|`x \| 2 > 3`|`x > 3`| |`<` / `>=`|`\|` / `^`|`x ^ 1 < 4` |`x < 4`|
rules.whyBad: Not equally evil as [`bad_bit_mask`](#bad_bit_mask), but still a bit misleading, because the bit mask is ineffective.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks if both `.write(true)` and `.append(true)` methods are called on a same `OpenOptions`.
rules.whyBad: `.append(true)` already enables `write(true)`, making this one superfluous.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for iteration that is guaranteed to be infinite.
rules.whyBad: While there may be places where this is acceptable (e.g., in event streams), in most cases this is simply an error.
rules.exampleBad:
use std::iter;

iter::repeat(1_u8).collect::<Vec<_>>();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for the definition of inherent methods with a signature of `to_string(&self) -> String` and if the type implementing this method also implements the `Display` trait.
rules.whyBad: This method is also implicitly defined if a type implements the `Display` trait. The less versatile inherent method will then shadow the implementation introduced by `Display`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `#[inline]` on trait methods without bodies
rules.whyBad: Only implementations of trait methods may be inlined. The inline attribute is ignored for trait methods without bodies.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `inspect().for_each()`.
rules.whyBad: It is the same as performing the computation inside `inspect` at the beginning of the closure in `for_each`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `x >= y + 1` or `x - 1 >= y` (and `<=`) in a block
rules.whyBad: Readability -- better to use `> y` instead of `>= y + 1`.
rules.exampleBad: if x >= y + 1 {}

rules.whatItDoes: This lint checks for invalid usages of `ptr::null`.
rules.whyBad: This causes undefined behavior.
rules.exampleBad:
// Undefined behavior
unsafe { std::slice::from_raw_parts(ptr::null(), 0); }

rules.whatItDoes: Checks [regex](https://crates.io/crates/regex) creation (with `Regex::new`, `RegexBuilder::new`, or `RegexSet::new`) for correct regex syntax.
rules.whyBad: This will lead to a runtime panic.
rules.exampleBad: Regex::new("(")

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for invisible Unicode characters in the code.
rules.whyBad: Having an invisible character in the code makes for all sorts of April fools, but otherwise is very much frowned upon.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for the use of `.iter().count()`.
rules.whyBad: `.len()` is more efficient and more readable.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for iterating a map (`HashMap` or `BTreeMap`) and ignoring either the keys or values.
rules.whyBad: Readability. There are `keys` and `values` methods that can be used to express that we only need the keys or the values.
rules.exampleBad:
let map: HashMap<u32, u32> = HashMap::new();
let values = map.iter().map(|(_, value)| value).collect::<Vec<_>>();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for loops on `x.next()`.
rules.whyBad: `next()` returns either `Some(value)` if there was a value, or `None` otherwise. The insidious thing is that `Option<_>` implements `IntoIterator`, so that possibly one value will be iterated, leading to some hard to find bugs. No one will want to write such code [except to win an Underhanded Rust Contest](https://www.reddit.com/r/rust/comments/3hb0wm/underhanded_rust_contest/cu5yuhr).

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for iterator combinator calls such as `.take(x)` or `.skip(x)` where `x` is greater than the amount of items that an iterator will produce.
rules.whyBad: Taking or skipping more items than there are in an iterator either creates an iterator with all items from the original iterator or an iterator with no items at all. This is most likely not what the user intended to do.
rules.exampleBad: for _ in [1, 2, 3].iter().take(4) {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.cloned().<func>()` where call to `.cloned()` can be postponed.
rules.whyBad: It's often inefficient to clone all elements of an iterator, when eventually, only some of them will be consumed.
rules.exampleBad:
vec.iter().cloned().take(10);
vec.iter().cloned().last();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `.skip(0)` on iterators.
rules.whyBad: This was likely intended to be `.skip(1)` to skip the first element, as `.skip(0)` does nothing. If not, the call should be removed.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calling `.step_by(0)` on iterators which panics.
rules.whyBad: This very much looks like an oversight. Use `panic!()` instead if you actually intend to panic.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `Path::join` that start with a path separator (`\\` or `/`).
rules.whyBad: If the argument to `Path::join` starts with a separator, it will overwrite the original path. If this is intentional, prefer using `Path::new` instead. Note the behavior is platform dependent. A leading `\\` will be accepted on unix systems as part of the file name See [`Path::join`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/path/struct.Path.html#method.join)

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for large `const` arrays that should be defined as `static` instead.
rules.whyBad: Performance: const variables are inlined upon use. Static items result in only one instance and has a fixed location in memory.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for large size differences between variants on `enum`s.
rules.whyBad: Enum size is bounded by the largest variant. Having one large variant can penalize the memory layout of that enum.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `let _ = <expr>` where the resulting type of expr implements `Future`
rules.whyBad: Futures must be polled for work to be done. The original intention was most likely to await the future and ignore the resulting value.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `let _ = sync_lock`. This supports `mutex` and `rwlock` in `parking_lot`. For `std` locks see the `rustc` lint [`let_underscore_lock`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/rustc/lints/listing/deny-by-default.html#let-underscore-lock)
rules.whyBad: This statement immediately drops the lock instead of extending its lifetime to the end of the scope, which is often not intended. To extend lock lifetime to the end of the scope, use an underscore-prefixed name instead (i.e. _lock). If you want to explicitly drop the lock, `std::mem::drop` conveys your intention better and is less error-prone.
rules.exampleBad: let _ = mutex.lock();

rules.whatItDoes: Detects when a variable is declared with an explicit type of `_`.
rules.whyBad: It adds noise, `: _` provides zero clarity or utility.
rules.exampleBad: let my_number: _ = 1;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `lines.filter_map(Result::ok)` or `lines.flat_map(Result::ok)` when `lines` has type `std::io::Lines`.
rules.whyBad: `Lines` instances might produce a never-ending stream of `Err`, in which case `filter_map(Result::ok)` will enter an infinite loop while waiting for an `Ok` variant. Calling `next()` once is sufficient to enter the infinite loop, even in the absence of explicit loops in the user code. This situation can arise when working with user-provided paths. On some platforms, `std::fs::File::open(path)` might return `Ok(fs)` even when `path` is a directory, but any later attempt to read from `fs` will return an error.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for lint groups with the same priority as lints in the `Cargo.toml` [`[lints]` table](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/manifest.html#the-lints-section). This lint will be removed once [cargo#12918](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/12918) is resolved.
rules.whyBad: The order of lints in the `[lints]` is ignored, to have a lint override a group the `priority` field needs to be used, otherwise the sort order is undefined.
rules.exampleBad:
[lints.clippy]
pedantic = "warn"
similar_names = "allow"

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for macros that expand metavariables in an unsafe block.
rules.whyBad: This hides an unsafe block and allows the user of the macro to write unsafe code without an explicit unsafe block at callsite, making it possible to perform unsafe operations in seemingly safe code. The macro should be restructured so that these metavariables are referenced outside of unsafe blocks and that the usual unsafety checks apply to the macro argument. This is usually done by binding it to a variable outside of the unsafe block and then using that variable inside of the block as shown in the example, or by referencing it a second time in a safe context, e.g. `if false { $expr }`.

rules.whatItDoes: Identifies good opportunities for a clamp function from std or core, and suggests using it.
rules.whyBad: clamp is much shorter, easier to read, and doesn't use any control flow.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `match` which could be implemented using `filter`
rules.whyBad: Using the `filter` method is clearer and more concise.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.filter(_).map(_)` that can be written more simply as `filter_map(_)`.
rules.whyBad: Redundant code in the `filter` and `map` operations is poor style and less performant.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for manual implementations of Iterator::find
rules.whyBad: It doesn't affect performance, but using `find` is shorter and easier to read.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.find(_).map(_)` that can be written more simply as `find_map(_)`.
rules.whyBad: Redundant code in the `find` and `map` operations is poor style and less performant.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for unnecessary `if let` usage in a for loop where only the `Some` or `Ok` variant of the iterator element is used.
rules.whyBad: It is verbose and can be simplified by first calling the `flatten` method on the `Iterator`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for cases where [`BuildHasher::hash_one`] can be used. [`BuildHasher::hash_one`]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/hash/trait.BuildHasher.html#method.hash_one
rules.whyBad: It is more concise to use the `hash_one` method.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for uses of `map` which return the original item.
rules.whyBad: `inspect` is both clearer in intent and shorter.
rules.exampleBad: let x = Some(0).map(|x| { println!("{x}"); x });

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for references on `std::path::MAIN_SEPARATOR.to_string()` used to build a `&str`.
rules.whyBad: There exists a `std::path::MAIN_SEPARATOR_STR` which does not require an extra memory allocation.
rules.exampleBad: let s: &str = &std::path::MAIN_SEPARATOR.to_string();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for for-loops that manually copy items between slices that could be optimized by having a memcpy.
rules.whyBad: It is not as fast as a memcpy.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for combined OR patterns that are all contained in a specific range, e.g. `6 | 4 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 8` can be rewritten as `4..=9`.
rules.whyBad: Using an explicit range is more concise and easier to read.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for an expression like `((x % 4) + 4) % 4` which is a common manual reimplementation of `x.rem_euclid(4)`.
rules.whyBad: It's simpler and more readable.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for code to be replaced by `.retain()`.
rules.whyBad: `.retain()` is simpler and avoids needless allocation.

rules.whatItDoes: When `a` is `&[T]`, detect `a.len() * size_of::<T>()` and suggest `size_of_val(a)` instead.
rules.exampleBad: let newlen = data.len() * std::mem::size_of::<i32>();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `str::splitn(2, _)`
rules.whyBad: `split_once` is both clearer in intent and slightly more efficient.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for manual implementations of `str::repeat`
rules.whyBad: These are both harder to read, as well as less performant.
rules.exampleBad: let x: String = std::iter::repeat('x').take(10).collect();

rules.whatItDoes: Suggests using `strip_{prefix,suffix}` over `str::{starts,ends}_with` and slicing using the pattern's length.
rules.whyBad: Using `str:strip_{prefix,suffix}` is safer and may have better performance as there is no slicing which may panic and the compiler does not need to insert this panic code. It is also sometimes more readable as it removes the need for duplicating or storing the pattern used by `str::{starts,ends}_with` and in the slicing.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for manual swapping. Note that the lint will not be emitted in const blocks, as the suggestion would not be applicable.
rules.whyBad: The `std::mem::swap` function exposes the intent better without deinitializing or copying either variable.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `Iterator::fold` with a type that implements `Try`.
rules.whyBad: The code should use `try_fold` instead, which short-circuits on failure, thus opening the door for additional optimizations not possible with `fold` as rustc can guarantee the function is never called on `None`, `Err`, etc., alleviating otherwise necessary checks. It's also slightly more idiomatic.
rules.exampleBad: vec![1, 2, 3].iter().fold(Some(0i32), |sum, i| sum?.checked_add(*i));

rules.whatItDoes: Finds patterns that reimplement `Option::unwrap_or` or `Result::unwrap_or`.
rules.whyBad: Concise code helps focusing on behavior instead of boilerplate.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks if a `match` or `if let` expression can be simplified using `.unwrap_or_default()`.
rules.whyBad: It can be done in one call with `.unwrap_or_default()`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `contains_key` + `insert` on `HashMap` or `BTreeMap`.
rules.whyBad: Using `entry` is more efficient.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.map(_).flatten(_)` on `Iterator` and `Option`
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely as `_.flat_map(_)` for `Iterator` or `_.and_then(_)` for `Option`

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for instances of `map(f)` where `f` is the identity function.
rules.whyBad: It can be written more concisely without the call to `map`.
rules.exampleBad:
let x = [1, 2, 3];
let y: Vec<_> = x.iter().map(|x| x).map(|x| 2*x).collect();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for match which is used to add a reference to an `Option` value.
rules.whyBad: Using `as_ref()` or `as_mut()` instead is shorter.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for useless match that binds to only one value.
rules.whyBad: Readability and needless complexity.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `match` expressions modifying the case of a string with non-compliant arms
rules.whyBad: The arm is unreachable, which is likely a mistake

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `mem::replace(&mut _, mem::uninitialized())` and `mem::replace(&mut _, mem::zeroed())`.
rules.whyBad: This will lead to undefined behavior even if the value is overwritten later, because the uninitialized value may be observed in the case of a panic.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for expressions where `std::cmp::min` and `max` are used to clamp values, but switched so that the result is constant.
rules.whyBad: This is in all probability not the intended outcome. At the least it hurts readability of the code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for getter methods that return a field that doesn't correspond to the name of the method, when there is a field's whose name matches that of the method.
rules.whyBad: It is most likely that such a method is a bug caused by a typo or by copy-pasting.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `a op= a op b` or `a op= b op a` patterns.
rules.whyBad: Most likely these are bugs where one meant to write `a op= b`.

rules.whatItDoes: Suggests to use `const` in `thread_local!` macro if possible.
rules.whyBad: The `thread_local!` macro wraps static declarations and makes them thread-local. It supports using a `const` keyword that may be used for declarations that can be evaluated as a constant expression. This can enable a more efficient thread local implementation that can avoid lazy initialization. For types that do not need to be dropped, this can enable an even more efficient implementation that does not need to track any additional state. https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.thread_local.html

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for empty spin loops
rules.whyBad: The loop body should have something like `thread::park()` or at least `std::hint::spin_loop()` to avoid needlessly burning cycles and conserve energy. Perhaps even better use an actual lock, if possible.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks if transmute calls have all generics specified.
rules.whyBad: If not set, some unexpected output type could be retrieved instead of the expected one, potentially leading to invalid code. This is particularly dangerous in case a seemingly innocent/unrelated change can cause type inference to start inferring a different type. E.g. the transmute is the tail expression of an `if` branch, and a different branches type changes, causing the transmute to silently have a different type, instead of a proper error.
rules.exampleBad: let x: i32 = std::mem::transmute([1u16, 2u16]);

rules.whatItDoes: Warns for mistyped suffix in literals
rules.whyBad: This is most probably a typo

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for getting the remainder of integer division by one or minus one.
rules.whyBad: The result for a divisor of one can only ever be zero; for minus one it can cause panic/overflow (if the left operand is the minimal value of the respective integer type) or results in zero. No one will write such code deliberately, unless trying to win an Underhanded Rust Contest. Even for that contest, it's probably a bad idea. Use something more underhanded.
rules.exampleBad:
let a = x % 1;
let a = x % -1;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for nested assignments.
rules.whyBad: While this is in most cases already a type mismatch, the result of an assignment being `()` can throw off people coming from languages like python or C, where such assignments return a copy of the assigned value.

rules.whatItDoes: Check if a generic is defined both in the bound predicate and in the `where` clause.
rules.whyBad: It can be confusing for developers when seeing bounds for a generic in multiple places.

rules.whatItDoes: This lint checks for functions that take immutable references and return mutable ones. This will not trigger if no unsafe code exists as there are multiple safe functions which will do this transformation To be on the conservative side, if there's at least one mutable reference with the output lifetime, this lint will not trigger.
rules.whyBad: Creating a mutable reference which can be repeatably derived from an immutable reference is unsound as it allows creating multiple live mutable references to the same object. This [error](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/39465) actually lead to an interim Rust release 1.15.1.
rules.exampleBad: fn foo(&Foo) -> &mut Bar { .. }

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for loops with a range bound that is a mutable variable.
rules.whyBad: One might think that modifying the mutable variable changes the loop bounds. It doesn't.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for sets/maps with mutable key types.
rules.whyBad: All of `HashMap`, `HashSet`, `BTreeMap` and `BtreeSet` rely on either the hash or the order of keys be unchanging, so having types with interior mutability is a bad idea.

rules.whatItDoes: The lint checks for `self` in fn parameters that specify the `Self`-type explicitly
rules.whyBad: Increases the amount and decreases the readability of code

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for expressions of the form `if c { true } else { false }` (or vice versa) and suggests using the condition directly.
rules.whyBad: Redundant code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for expressions of the form `if c { x = true } else { x = false }` (or vice versa) and suggest assigning the variable directly from the condition.
rules.whyBad: Redundant code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for bindings that needlessly destructure a reference and borrow the inner value with `&ref`.
rules.whyBad: This pattern has no effect in almost all cases.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks if an iterator is used to check if a string is ascii.
rules.whyBad: The `str` type already implements the `is_ascii` method.
rules.exampleBad: "foo".chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii());

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for empty `if` branches with no else branch.
rules.whyBad: It can be entirely omitted, and often the condition too.
rules.exampleBad:
if really_expensive_condition(&i) {}
if really_expensive_condition_with_side_effects(&mut i) {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for lifetime annotations which can be removed by relying on lifetime elision.
rules.whyBad: The additional lifetimes make the code look more complicated, while there is nothing out of the ordinary going on. Removing them leads to more readable code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for unnecessary `match` or match-like `if let` returns for `Option` and `Result` when function signatures are the same.
rules.whyBad: This `match` block does nothing and might not be what the coder intended.

rules.whatItDoes: Lints `?Sized` bounds applied to type parameters that cannot be unsized
rules.whyBad: The `?Sized` bound is misleading because it cannot be satisfied by an unsized type
rules.exampleBad:
// `T` cannot be unsized because `Clone` requires it to be `Sized`
fn f<T: Clone + ?Sized>(t: &T) {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for no-op uses of `Option::{as_deref, as_deref_mut}`, for example, `Option<&T>::as_deref()` returns the same type.
rules.whyBad: Redundant code and improving readability.
rules.exampleBad:
let a = Some(&1);
let b = a.as_deref(); // goes from Option<&i32> to Option<&i32>

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calling `take` function after `as_ref`.
rules.whyBad: Redundant code. `take` writes `None` to its argument. In this case the modification is useless as it's a temporary that cannot be read from afterwards.
rules.exampleBad:
let x = Some(3);
x.as_ref().take();

rules.whatItDoes: Suggests alternatives for useless applications of `?` in terminating expressions
rules.whyBad: There's no reason to use `?` to short-circuit when execution of the body will end there anyway.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `str::splitn` (or `str::rsplitn`) where using `str::split` would be the same.
rules.whyBad: The function `split` is simpler and there is no performance difference in these cases, considering that both functions return a lazy iterator.
rules.exampleBad:
let str = "key=value=add";
let _ = str.splitn(3, '=').next().unwrap();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for needlessly including a base struct on update when all fields are changed anyway. This lint is not applied to structs marked with [non_exhaustive](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/attributes/type_system.html).
rules.whyBad: This will cost resources (because the base has to be somewhere), and make the code less readable.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for the usage of negated comparison operators on types which only implement `PartialOrd` (e.g., `f64`).
rules.whyBad: These operators make it easy to forget that the underlying types actually allow not only three potential Orderings (Less, Equal, Greater) but also a fourth one (Uncomparable). This is especially easy to miss if the operator based comparison result is negated.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for loops that will always `break`, `return` or `continue` an outer loop.
rules.whyBad: This loop never loops, all it does is obfuscating the code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for statements which have no effect.
rules.whyBad: Unlike dead code, these statements are actually executed. However, as they have no effect, all they do is make the code less readable.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `replace` statements which have no effect.
rules.whyBad: It's either a mistake or confusing.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for non-canonical implementations of `Clone` when `Copy` is already implemented.
rules.whyBad: If both `Clone` and `Copy` are implemented, they must agree. This can done by dereferencing `self` in `Clone`'s implementation, which will avoid any possibility of the implementations becoming out of sync.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for non-canonical implementations of `PartialOrd` when `Ord` is already implemented.
rules.whyBad: If both `PartialOrd` and `Ord` are implemented, they must agree. This is commonly done by wrapping the result of `cmp` in `Some` for `partial_cmp`. Not doing this may silently introduce an error upon refactoring.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for non-octal values used to set Unix file permissions.
rules.whyBad: They will be converted into octal, creating potentially unintended file permissions.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for boolean expressions that can be written more concisely.
rules.whyBad: Readability of boolean expressions suffers from unnecessary duplication.
rules.exampleBad:
if a && true {}
if !(a == b) {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for duplicate open options as well as combinations that make no sense.
rules.whyBad: In the best case, the code will be harder to read than necessary. I don't know the worst case.
rules.exampleBad:
use std::fs::OpenOptions;

OpenOptions::new().read(true).truncate(true);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for public functions that dereference raw pointer arguments but are not marked `unsafe`.
rules.whyBad: The function should almost definitely be marked `unsafe`, since for an arbitrary raw pointer, there is no way of telling for sure if it is valid. In general, this lint should **never be disabled** unless it is definitely a false positive (please submit an issue if so) since it breaks Rust's soundness guarantees, directly exposing API users to potentially dangerous program behavior. This is also true for internal APIs, as it is easy to leak unsoundness.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `\0` escapes in string and byte literals that look like octal character escapes in C.
rules.whyBad: C and other languages support octal character escapes in strings, where a backslash is followed by up to three octal digits. For example, `\033` stands for the ASCII character 27 (ESC). Rust does not support this notation, but has the escape code `\0` which stands for a null byte/character, and any following digits do not form part of the escape sequence. Therefore, `\033` is not a compiler error but the result may be surprising.
rules.exampleBad:
let one = "\033[1m Bold? \033[0m";  // \033 intended as escape
let two = "\033\0";                 // \033 intended as null-3-3

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for arguments that are only used in recursion with no side-effects.
rules.whyBad: It could contain a useless calculation and can make function simpler. The arguments can be involved in calculations and assignments but as long as the calculations have no side-effects (function calls or mutating dereference) and the assigned variables are also only in recursion, it is useless.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.as_ref().map(Deref::deref)` or its aliases (such as String::as_str).
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely as `_.as_deref()`.
rules.exampleBad: opt.as_ref().map(String::as_str)

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `option_env!(...).unwrap()` and suggests usage of the `env!` macro.
rules.whyBad: Unwrapping the result of `option_env!` will panic at run-time if the environment variable doesn't exist, whereas `env!` catches it at compile-time.
rules.exampleBad: let _ = option_env!("HOME").unwrap();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for iterators of `Option`s using `.filter(Option::is_some).map(Option::unwrap)` that may be replaced with a `.flatten()` call.
rules.whyBad: `Option` is like a collection of 0-1 things, so `flatten` automatically does this without suspicious-looking `unwrap` calls.
rules.exampleBad: let _ = std::iter::empty::<Option<i32>>().filter(Option::is_some).map(Option::unwrap);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `option.map(f)` where f is a function or closure that returns the unit type `()`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more clearly with an if let statement

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `.or(…).unwrap()` calls to Options and Results.
rules.whyBad: You should use `.unwrap_or(…)` instead for clarity.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for out of bounds array indexing with a constant index.
rules.whyBad: This will always panic at runtime.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for boolean expressions that contain terminals that can be eliminated.
rules.whyBad: This is most likely a logic bug.
rules.exampleBad:
// The `b` is unnecessary, the expression is equivalent to `if a`.
if a && b || a { ... }

rules.whatItDoes: Detects C-style underflow/overflow checks.
rules.whyBad: These checks will, by default, panic in debug builds rather than check whether the operation caused an overflow.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls of `unwrap[_err]()` that will always fail.
rules.whyBad: If panicking is desired, an explicit `panic!()` should be used.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for manual re-implementations of `PartialEq::ne`.
rules.whyBad: `PartialEq::ne` is required to always return the negated result of `PartialEq::eq`, which is exactly what the default implementation does. Therefore, there should never be any need to re-implement it.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for calls to `Path::ends_with` calls where the argument looks like a file extension. By default, Clippy has a short list of known filenames that start with a dot but aren't necessarily file extensions (e.g. the `.git` folder), which are allowed by default. The `allowed-dotfiles` configuration can be used to allow additional file extensions that Clippy should not lint.
rules.whyBad: This doesn't actually compare file extensions. Rather, `ends_with` compares the given argument to the last **component** of the path and checks if it matches exactly.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `std::fs::Permissions.set_readonly` with argument `false`.
rules.whyBad: On Unix platforms this results in the file being world writable, equivalent to `chmod a+w <file>`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for possible missing comma in an array. It lints if an array element is a binary operator expression and it lies on two lines.
rules.whyBad: This could lead to unexpected results.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for operations where precedence may be unclear and suggests to add parentheses. Currently it catches the following: * mixed usage of arithmetic and bit shifting/combining operators without parentheses
rules.whyBad: Not everyone knows the precedence of those operators by heart, so expressions like these may trip others trying to reason about the code.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `println`, `print`, `eprintln` or `eprint` in an implementation of a formatting trait.
rules.whyBad: Using a print macro is likely unintentional since formatting traits should write to the `Formatter`, not stdout/stderr.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of the `offset` pointer method with a `usize` casted to an `isize`.
rules.whyBad: If we’re always increasing the pointer address, we can avoid the numeric cast by using the `add` method instead.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for zipping a collection with the range of `0.._.len()`.
rules.whyBad: The code is better expressed with `.enumerate()`.
rules.exampleBad: let _ = x.iter().zip(0..x.len());

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for reference-counted pointers (`Arc`, `Rc`, `rc::Weak`, and `sync::Weak`) in `vec![elem; len]`
rules.whyBad: This will create `elem` once and clone it `len` times - doing so with `Arc`/`Rc`/`Weak` is a bit misleading, as it will create references to the same pointer, rather than different instances.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for calls to [`Stdin::read_line`] to read a line from the standard input into a string, then later attempting to use that string for an operation that will never work for strings with a trailing newline character in it (e.g. parsing into a `i32`).
rules.whyBad: The operation will always fail at runtime no matter what the user enters, thus making it a useless operation.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for calls to `RwLock::write` where the lock is only used for reading.
rules.whyBad: The write portion of `RwLock` is exclusive, meaning that no other thread can access the lock while this writer is active.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for format trait implementations (e.g. `Display`) with a recursive call to itself which uses `self` as a parameter. This is typically done indirectly with the `write!` macro or with `to_string()`.
rules.whyBad: This will lead to infinite recursion and a stack overflow.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of redundant allocations anywhere in the code.
rules.whyBad: Expressions such as `Rc<&T>`, `Rc<Rc<T>>`, `Rc<Arc<T>>`, `Rc<Box<T>>`, `Arc<&T>`, `Arc<Rc<T>>`, `Arc<Arc<T>>`, `Arc<Box<T>>`, `Box<&T>`, `Box<Rc<T>>`, `Box<Arc<T>>`, `Box<Box<T>>`, add an unnecessary level of indirection.
rules.exampleBad: fn foo(bar: Rc<&usize>) {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `as_str()` on a `String` chained with a method available on the `String` itself.
rules.whyBad: The `as_str()` conversion is pointless and can be removed for simplicity and cleanliness.
rules.exampleBad:
let owned_string = "This is a string".to_owned();
owned_string.as_str().as_bytes()

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `async` block that only returns `await` on a future.
rules.whyBad: It is simpler and more efficient to use the future directly.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `[all @ ..]` patterns.
rules.whyBad: In all cases, `all` works fine and can often make code simpler, as you possibly won't need to convert from say a `Vec` to a slice by dereferencing.

rules.whatItDoes: Detects closures called in the same expression where they are defined.
rules.whyBad: It is unnecessarily adding to the expression's complexity.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for ineffective double comparisons against constants.
rules.whyBad: Only one of the comparisons has any effect on the result, the programmer probably intended to flip one of the comparison operators, or compare a different value entirely.
rules.exampleBad: if status_code <= 400 && status_code < 500 {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for unnecessary guards in match expressions.
rules.whyBad: It's more complex and much less readable. Making it part of the pattern can improve exhaustiveness checking as well.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for redundant redefinitions of local bindings.
rules.whyBad: Redundant redefinitions of local bindings do not change behavior and are likely to be unintended. Note that although these bindings do not affect your code's meaning, they _may_ affect `rustc`'s stack allocation.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for redundant slicing expressions which use the full range, and do not change the type.
rules.whyBad: It unnecessarily adds complexity to the expression.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `.repeat(1)` and suggest the following method for each types. - `.to_string()` for `str` - `.clone()` for `String` - `.to_vec()` for `slice` The lint will evaluate constant expressions and values as arguments of `.repeat(..)` and emit a message if they are equivalent to `1`. (Related discussion in [rust-clippy#7306](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/7306))
rules.whyBad: For example, `String.repeat(1)` is equivalent to `.clone()`. If cloning the string is the intention behind this, `clone()` should be used.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for patterns such as `vec![Vec::with_capacity(x); n]` or `iter::repeat(Vec::with_capacity(x))`.
rules.whyBad: These constructs work by cloning the element, but cloning a `Vec<_>` does not respect the old vector's capacity and effectively discards it. This makes `iter::repeat(Vec::with_capacity(x))` especially suspicious because the user most certainly expected that the yielded `Vec<_>` will have the requested capacity, otherwise one can simply write `iter::repeat(Vec::new())` instead and it will have the same effect. Similarly for `vec![x; n]`, the element `x` is cloned to fill the vec. Unlike `iter::repeat` however, the vec repeat macro does not have to clone the value `n` times but just `n - 1` times, because it can reuse the passed value for the last slot. That means that the last `Vec<_>` gets the requested capacity but all other ones do not.
rules.exampleBad:
let _: Vec<Vec<u8>> = vec![Vec::with_capacity(42); 123];
let _: Vec<Vec<u8>> = iter::repeat(Vec::with_capacity(42)).take(123).collect();

rules.whatItDoes: Informs the user about a more concise way to create a vector with a known capacity.
rules.whyBad: The `Vec::with_capacity` constructor is less complex.
rules.exampleBad:
let mut v: Vec<usize> = vec![];
v.reserve(10);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for iterators of `Result`s using `.filter(Result::is_ok).map(Result::unwrap)` that may be replaced with a `.flatten()` call.
rules.whyBad: `Result` implements `IntoIterator<Item = T>`. This means that `Result` can be flattened automatically without suspicious-looking `unwrap` calls.
rules.exampleBad: let _ = std::iter::empty::<Result<i32, ()>>().filter(Result::is_ok).map(Result::unwrap);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for functions that return `Result` with an unusually large `Err`-variant.
rules.whyBad: A `Result` is at least as large as the `Err`-variant. While we expect that variant to be seldom used, the compiler needs to reserve and move that much memory every single time. Furthermore, errors are often simply passed up the call-stack, making use of the `?`-operator and its type-conversion mechanics. If the `Err`-variant further up the call-stack stores the `Err`-variant in question (as library code often does), it itself needs to be at least as large, propagating the problem.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `result.map(f)` where f is a function or closure that returns the unit type `()`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more clearly with an if let statement

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for range expressions `x..y` where both `x` and `y` are constant and `x` is greater to `y`. Also triggers if `x` is equal to `y` when they are conditions to a `for` loop.
rules.whyBad: Empty ranges yield no values so iterating them is a no-op. Moreover, trying to use a reversed range to index a slice will panic at run-time.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for an iterator or string search (such as `find()`, `position()`, or `rposition()`) followed by a call to `is_some()` or `is_none()`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely as: * `_.any(_)`, or `_.contains(_)` for `is_some()`, * `!_.any(_)`, or `!_.contains(_)` for `is_none()`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks if the `seek` method of the `Seek` trait is called with `SeekFrom::Current(0)`, and if it is, suggests using `stream_position` instead.
rules.whyBad: Readability. Use dedicated method.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for jumps to the start of a stream that implements `Seek` and uses the `seek` method providing `Start` as parameter.
rules.whyBad: Readability. There is a specific method that was implemented for this exact scenario.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for explicit self-assignments.
rules.whyBad: Self-assignments are redundant and unlikely to be intentional.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for misuses of the serde API.
rules.whyBad: Serde is very finicky about how its API should be used, but the type system can't be used to enforce it (yet?).

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for the use of short circuit boolean conditions as a statement.
rules.whyBad: Using a short circuit boolean condition as a statement may hide the fact that the second part is executed or not depending on the outcome of the first part.
rules.exampleBad: f() && g(); // We should write `if f() { g(); }`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks whether a for loop has a single element.
rules.whyBad: There is no reason to have a loop of a single element.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `Vec` or array initializations that contain only one range.
rules.whyBad: This is almost always incorrect, as it will result in a `Vec` that has only one element. Almost always, the programmer intended for it to include all elements in the range or for the end of the range to be the length instead.

rules.whatItDoes: Detects expressions where `size_of::<T>` or `size_of_val::<T>` is used as a count of elements of type `T`
rules.whyBad: These functions expect a count of `T` and not a number of bytes

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `std::mem::size_of_val()` where the argument is a reference to a reference.
rules.whyBad: Calling `size_of_val()` with a reference to a reference as the argument yields the size of the reference-type, not the size of the value behind the reference.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `_.skip_while(condition).next()`.
rules.whyBad: Readability, this can be written more concisely as `_.find(!condition)`.
rules.exampleBad: vec.iter().skip_while(|x| **x == 0).next();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks slow zero-filled vector initialization
rules.whyBad: These structures are non-idiomatic and less efficient than simply using `vec![0; len]`. Specifically, for `vec![0; len]`, the compiler can use a specialized type of allocation that also zero-initializes the allocated memory in the same call (see: [alloc_zeroed](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/alloc/trait.GlobalAlloc.html#method.alloc_zeroed)). Writing `Vec::new()` followed by `vec.resize(len, 0)` is suboptimal because, while it does do the same number of allocations, it involves two operations for allocating and initializing. The `resize` call first allocates memory (since `Vec::new()` did not), and only *then* zero-initializes it.

rules.whatItDoes: Check if the string is transformed to byte array and casted back to string.
rules.whyBad: It's unnecessary, the string can be used directly.
rules.exampleBad: std::str::from_utf8(&"Hello World!".as_bytes()[6..11]).unwrap();

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of `libc::strlen` on a `CString` or `CStr` value, and suggest calling `as_bytes().len()` or `to_bytes().len()` respectively instead.
rules.whyBad: This avoids calling an unsafe `libc` function. Currently, it also avoids calculating the length.

rules.whatItDoes: Lints for suspicious operations in impls of arithmetic operators, e.g. subtracting elements in an Add impl.
rules.whyBad: This is probably a typo or copy-and-paste error and not intended.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for usage of the non-existent `=*`, `=!` and `=-` operators.
rules.whyBad: This is either a typo of `*=`, `!=` or `-=` or confusing.
rules.exampleBad: a =- 42; // confusing, should it be `a -= 42` or `a = -42`?

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `Command::arg()` invocations that look like they should be multiple arguments instead, such as `arg("-t ext2")`.
rules.whyBad: `Command::arg()` does not split arguments by space. An argument like `arg("-t ext2")` will be passed as a single argument to the command, which is likely not what was intended.
rules.exampleBad: std::process::Command::new("echo").arg("-n hello").spawn().unwrap();

rules.whatItDoes: Detects the use of outer doc comments (`///`, `/**`) followed by a bang (`!`): `///!`
rules.whyBad: Triple-slash comments (known as "outer doc comments") apply to items that follow it. An outer doc comment followed by a bang (i.e. `///!`) has no specific meaning. The user most likely meant to write an inner doc comment (`//!`, `/*!`), which applies to the parent item (i.e. the item that the comment is contained in, usually a module or crate).

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for formatting of `else`. It lints if the `else` is followed immediately by a newline or the `else` seems to be missing.
rules.whyBad: This is probably some refactoring remnant, even if the code is correct, it might look confusing.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `map` followed by a `count`.
rules.whyBad: It looks suspicious. Maybe `map` was confused with `filter`. If the `map` call is intentional, this should be rewritten using `inspect`. Or, if you intend to drive the iterator to completion, you can just use `for_each` instead.
rules.exampleBad: let _ = (0..3).map(|x| x + 2).count();

rules.whatItDoes: Lints for suspicious operations in impls of OpAssign, e.g. subtracting elements in an AddAssign impl.
rules.whyBad: This is probably a typo or copy-and-paste error and not intended.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for the suspicious use of `OpenOptions::create()` without an explicit `OpenOptions::truncate()`.
rules.whyBad: `create()` alone will either create a new file or open an existing file. If the file already exists, it will be overwritten when written to, but the file will not be truncated by default. If less data is written to the file than it already contains, the remainder of the file will remain unchanged, and the end of the file will contain old data. In most cases, one should either use `create_new` to ensure the file is created from scratch, or ensure `truncate` is called so that the truncation behaviour is explicit. `truncate(true)` will ensure the file is entirely overwritten with new data, whereas `truncate(false)` will explicitly keep the default behavior.
rules.exampleBad:
use std::fs::OpenOptions;

OpenOptions::new().create(true);

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to [`splitn`] (https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html#method.splitn) and related functions with either zero or one splits.
rules.whyBad: These calls don't actually split the value and are likely to be intended as a different number.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for the usage of `_.to_owned()`, on a `Cow<'_, _>`.
rules.whyBad: Calling `to_owned()` on a `Cow` creates a clone of the `Cow` itself, without taking ownership of the `Cow` contents (i.e. it's equivalent to calling `Cow::clone`). The similarly named `into_owned` method, on the other hand, clones the `Cow` contents, effectively turning any `Cow::Borrowed` into a `Cow::Owned`. Given the potential ambiguity, consider replacing `to_owned` with `clone` for better readability or, if getting a `Cow::Owned` was the original intent, using `into_owned` instead.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks the formatting of a unary operator on the right hand side of a binary operator. It lints if there is no space between the binary and unary operators, but there is a space between the unary and its operand.
rules.whyBad: This is either a typo in the binary operator or confusing.
rules.exampleBad:
// &&! looks like a different operator
if foo &&! bar {}

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for calls to `core::mem::swap` where either parameter is derived from a pointer
rules.whyBad: When at least one parameter to `swap` is derived from a pointer it may overlap with the other. This would then lead to undefined behavior.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for construction of a structure or tuple just to assign a value in it.
rules.whyBad: Readability. If the structure is only created to be updated, why not write the structure you want in the first place?

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `#[test]` in doctests unless they are marked with either `ignore`, `no_run` or `compile_fail`.
rules.whyBad: Code in examples marked as `#[test]` will somewhat surprisingly not be run by `cargo test`. If you really want to show how to test stuff in an example, mark it `no_run` to make the intent clear.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for [`ToString::to_string`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/string/trait.ToString.html#tymethod.to_string) applied to a type that implements [`Display`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fmt/trait.Display.html) in a macro that does formatting.
rules.whyBad: Since the type implements `Display`, the use of `to_string` is unnecessary.
rules.exampleBad: println!("error: something failed at {}", Location::caller().to_string());

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for functions with too many parameters.
rules.whyBad: Functions with lots of parameters are considered bad style and reduce readability (“what does the 5th parameter mean?”). Consider grouping some parameters into a new type.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from a `&[u8]` to a `&str`.
rules.whyBad: Not every byte slice is a valid UTF-8 string.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from a float to an integer.
rules.whyBad: Transmutes are dangerous and error-prone, whereas `to_bits` is intuitive and safe.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from an integer to a `bool`.
rules.whyBad: This might result in an invalid in-memory representation of a `bool`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from an integer to a `char`.
rules.whyBad: Not every integer is a Unicode scalar value.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from an integer to a float.
rules.whyBad: Transmutes are dangerous and error-prone, whereas `from_bits` is intuitive and safe.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from `T` to `NonZero<T>`, and suggests the `new_unchecked` method instead.
rules.whyBad: Transmutes work on any types and thus might cause unsoundness when those types change elsewhere. `new_unchecked` only works for the appropriate types instead.
rules.exampleBad: let _: NonZero<u32> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(123) };

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for null function pointer creation through transmute.
rules.whyBad: Creating a null function pointer is undefined behavior. More info: https://doc.rust-lang.org/nomicon/ffi.html#the-nullable-pointer-optimization
rules.exampleBad: let null_fn: fn() = unsafe { std::mem::transmute( std::ptr::null::<()>() ) };

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes from a number to an array of `u8`
Checks for transmutes from a pointer to a reference.
rules.whyBad: This can always be rewritten with `&` and `*`.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmutes that could be a pointer cast.
rules.whyBad: Readability. The code tricks people into thinking that something complex is going on.
rules.exampleBad: unsafe { std::mem::transmute::<*const [i32], *const [u16]>(p) };

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for transmute calls which would receive a null pointer.
rules.whyBad: Transmuting a null pointer is undefined behavior.
rules.exampleBad: let null_ref: &u64 = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(0 as *const u64) };

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for types used in structs, parameters and `let` declarations above a certain complexity threshold.
rules.whyBad: Too complex types make the code less readable. Consider using a `type` definition to simplify them.

rules.whatItDoes: Looks for calls to `.type_id()` on a `Box<dyn _>`.
rules.whyBad: This almost certainly does not do what the user expects and can lead to subtle bugs. Calling `.type_id()` on a `Box<dyn Trait>` returns a fixed `TypeId` of the `Box` itself, rather than returning the `TypeId` of the underlying type behind the trait object. For `Box<dyn Any>` specifically (and trait objects that have `Any` as its supertrait), this lint will provide a suggestion, which is to dereference the receiver explicitly to go from `Box<dyn Any>` to `dyn Any`. This makes sure that `.type_id()` resolves to a dynamic call on the trait object and not on the box. If the fixed `TypeId` of the `Box` is the intended behavior, it's better to be explicit about it and write `TypeId::of::<Box<dyn Trait>>()`: this makes it clear that a fixed `TypeId` is returned and not the `TypeId` of the implementor.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks that there isn't an infinite recursion in trait implementations.
rules.whyBad: This is a hard to find infinite recursion that will crash any code using it.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `MaybeUninit::uninit().assume_init()`.
rules.whyBad: For most types, this is undefined behavior.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `set_len()` call that creates `Vec` with uninitialized elements. This is commonly caused by calling `set_len()` right after allocating or reserving a buffer with `new()`, `default()`, `with_capacity()`, or `reserve()`.
rules.whyBad: It creates a `Vec` with uninitialized data, which leads to undefined behavior with most safe operations. Notably, uninitialized `Vec<u8>` must not be used with generic `Read`. Moreover, calling `set_len()` on a `Vec` created with `new()` or `default()` creates out-of-bound values that lead to heap memory corruption when used.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for passing a unit value as an argument to a function without using a unit literal (`()`).
rules.whyBad: This is likely the result of an accidental semicolon.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for comparisons to unit. This includes all binary comparisons (like `==` and `<`) and asserts.
rules.whyBad: Unit is always equal to itself, and thus is just a clumsily written constant. Mostly this happens when someone accidentally adds semicolons at the end of the operands.

rules.whatItDoes: Detects `().hash(_)`.
rules.whyBad: Hashing a unit value doesn't do anything as the implementation of `Hash` for `()` is a no-op.

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for functions that expect closures of type Fn(...) -> Ord where the implemented closure returns the unit type. The lint also suggests to remove the semi-colon at the end of the statement if present.
rules.whyBad: Likely, returning the unit type is unintentional, and could simply be caused by an extra semi-colon. Since () implements Ord it doesn't cause a compilation error. This is the same reasoning behind the unit_cmp lint.
rules.exampleBad:
let mut twins = vec![(1, 1), (2, 2)];
twins.sort_by_key(|x| { x.1; });

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for casts to the same type, casts of int literals to integer types, casts of float literals to float types, and casts between raw pointers that don't change type or constness.
rules.whyBad: It's just unnecessary.
rules.exampleBad:
let _ = 2i32 as i32;
let _ = 0.5 as f32;

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `#[cfg_attr(clippy, allow(clippy::lint))]` and suggests to replace it with `#[allow(clippy::lint)]`.
rules.whyBad: There is no reason to put clippy attributes behind a clippy `cfg` as they are not run by anything else than clippy.
rules.exampleBad: #![cfg_attr(clippy, allow(clippy::deprecated_cfg_attr))]

rules.whatItDoes: Checks for `filter_map` calls that could be replaced by `filter` or `map`. More specifically it checks if the closure provided is only performing one of the filter or map operations and suggests the appropriate option.
